docs.title: sigrok-mcp-server
docs.text:
An MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that wraps sigrok-cli, exposing sigrok's signal analysis capabilities to LLMs.

It translates MCP tool calls into sigrok-cli invocations and returns structured JSON results, enabling LLMs to query logic analyzers, decode protocols, and analyze signals.

docs.title: What can it do?
docs.text:
• Discover hardware — Scan for connected logic analyzers, oscilloscopes, and multimeters
  • Capture data — Acquire signal data from devices and save to files
  • Decode protocols — Analyze captured data with 100+ protocol decoders (UART, SPI, I2C, etc.)
  • Query instruments — Send SCPI commands directly to serial-attached instruments
  • Browse capabilities — List supported drivers, decoders, and file formats

docs.title: Available Tools
docs.text: Tool Description list_supported_hardware List all supported hardware drivers list_supported_decoders List all supported protocol decoders list_input_formats List all supported input file formats list_output_formats List all supported output file formats show_decoder_details Show detailed info about a protocol decoder show_driver_details Show detailed info about a hardware driver show_version Show sigrok-cli and library version info scan_devices Scan for connected hardware devices capture_data Capture data from a device and save to file decode_protocol Decode protocol data from a captured file check_firmware_status Check firmware file availability serial_query Send commands over a serial port get_device_profile Look up device profiles and connection settings

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
A typical signal analysis workflow with an LLM:

  1. Discover — scan_devices to find connected hardware
  2. Inspect — show_driver_details to check driver capabilities
  3. Capture — capture_data to acquire signal data
  4. Decode — decode_protocol to analyze with protocol decoders
  5. Understand — The LLM interprets decoded output and explains the communication

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: See the Installation guide to get up and running.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
MCP Client (LLM)
    |  stdio (JSON-RPC)
    v
sigrok-mcp-server (Go)
    |  exec.Command
    v
sigrok-cli
    |
    v
libsigrok / libsigrokdecode
  • Transport: stdio (stdin/stdout JSON-RPC)
  • No C bindings: sigrok-cli is the sole interface to sigrok
  • Structured output: Raw sigrok-cli text output is parsed into JSON

docs.title: Package structure
docs.text:
cmd/sigrok-mcp-server/     Entry point
internal/
  config/                  Environment-based configuration
  sigrok/
    executor.go            sigrok-cli command execution with timeout
    parser.go              Output parsing (list, decoder, driver, version, scan)
    testdata/              Real sigrok-cli output fixtures
  serial/                  Serial port querier (independent of sigrok-cli)
  devices/                 Device profile registry with embedded JSON profiles
  tools/
    tools.go               MCP tool definitions and registration
    handlers.go            Tool handler implementations

docs.title: sigrok-cli as the sole interface
docs.text: The server does not use C bindings or link against libsigrok directly. Instead, it shells out to sigrok-cli for all sigrok operations. This simplifies the build (pure Go binary), avoids CGO complexity, and leverages the same CLI tool that sigrok users already know.

docs.title: Command injection prevention
docs.text: All user-provided inputs are validated with regexes before being passed to sigrok-cli. Filenames are restricted to flat names (no path separators) to prevent path traversal.

docs.title: Testability
docs.text:
• Runner interface in internal/tools/ enables mock-based handler tests
  • CommandFactory in internal/sigrok/executor.go is a test seam for injecting fake commands
  • Golden output files in internal/sigrok/testdata/ enable parser tests without sigrok-cli

docs.title: serial_query independence
docs.text: The serial_query and get_device_profile tools operate independently of sigrok-cli, using a pure Go serial library. This allows instrument communication even when sigrok's SCPI driver doesn't support the device.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Contributions to sigrok-mcp-server are welcome! This guide covers the development workflow, branching conventions, and code standards.

docs.title: Using the Dev Container (recommended)
docs.text:
The repository includes a dev container configuration. Open the project in VS Code or any IDE with dev container support, or use:

devcontainer up

docs.title: Local Setup
docs.text:
Requirements:

  • Go 1.23+
  • sigrok-cli (for integration testing)
# Build
go build ./...

# Test
go test ./... -race

# Lint
go vet ./...
golangci-lint run ./...

docs.title: Branch Naming
docs.text:
All branches must follow this naming convention:

<type>/<short-description>
Prefix Purpose Example feat/ New feature feat/serial-port-auto-detect fix/ Bug fix fix/timeout-handling perf/ Performance improvement perf/parser-allocation refactor/ Code refactoring refactor/executor-interface docs/ Documentation only docs/troubleshooting-guide ci/ CI/CD changes ci/add-lint-workflow chore/ Other maintenance chore/update-dependencies

Use kebab-case for <short-description> (e.g. feat/serial-port-auto-detect, not feat/serialPortAutoDetect).

docs.title: Commit Messages
docs.text:
Follow Conventional Commits style:

<type>: <description>

Examples:

feat: add serial port auto-detection
fix: handle timeout when device is unresponsive
docs: add OWON XDM1241 device profile guide
ci: add PR labeler workflow
chore: update mcp-go to v0.45.0
perf: reduce allocations in protocol decoder parser
refactor: extract command builder from executor

docs.title: Pull Requests
docs.text:
1. Create a branch from main following the branch naming convention
  2. Make your changes with tests
  3. Ensure go test ./... -race passes
  4. Ensure go vet ./... reports no issues
  5. Submit a pull request against main

docs.title: Automatic Labels
docs.text:
PRs are automatically labeled based on branch name and changed files:

By branch name:

Branch prefix Label feat/ Type: Feature fix/ Type: Bug perf/ Type: Enhancement refactor/, chore/, ci/ Type: House Keeping docs/ Type: Documentation

By changed files:

Files Label **/*.go, go.mod, go.sum go Dockerfile, .devcontainer/** docker .github/workflows/** github_actions go.mod, go.sum dependencies

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text:
• Follow standard Go conventions (gofmt, go vet)
  • Use table-driven tests with t.Run() subtests
  • Error handling: return errors, don't panic; wrap with fmt.Errorf("context: %w", err)
  • Naming: exported names in PascalCase, unexported in camelCase
  • Keep packages small and focused; avoid circular dependencies

docs.title: Areas for Contribution
docs.text:
• Device documentation: Test with your hardware and document the results in docs/devices/
  • Protocol decode examples: Share real-world decode workflows in docs/guides/
  • Bug fixes and improvements: Check open issues
  • Device profiles: Add JSON profiles for new instruments in internal/devices/

docs.title: Reporting Issues
docs.text: Please use GitHub Issues to report bugs or request features.

docs.title: Devices
docs.text: This section documents devices that have been tested with sigrok-mcp-server, including connection settings, verified commands, and device-specific notes.

docs.title: Tested Devices
docs.text: Device Type Connection Notes OWON XDM1241 Digital multimeter USB serial (CH340) Non-standard SCPI, use serial_query

docs.title: Adding device documentation
docs.text:
If you've tested a device with sigrok-mcp-server, contributions are welcome! See Contributing for how to submit device documentation.

A device page should include:

  • Overview and connection parameters
  • Verified commands and expected responses
  • Commands that do not work (to save others from troubleshooting)
  • Usage examples with the appropriate MCP tools

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The OWON XDM1241 is a 4 1/2 digit bench-type digital multimeter with USB serial connectivity. It is a variant of the XDM1041 (battery-powered, USB-C powered instead of AC mains). Internally it uses a CH340 USB-to-serial chip.

sigrok's scpi-dmm driver does not recognize this device. Use the serial_query MCP tool for direct SCPI communication.

docs.title: Connection Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Value Port /dev/ttyUSB0 (typical) Baud rate 115200 Data bits 8 Parity None Stop bits 1 Timeout 3000 ms (recommended)

docs.title: Command Conventions
docs.text:
The XDM1241 uses a non-standard, flat SCPI command set. Key differences from standard SCPI:

  • No subsystem hierarchy: Commands like MEAS:VOLT:DC?, VOLT:RANG?, or SYST:ERR? are not supported and will time out with no response.
  • Display suffix: Many commands use a numeric suffix (1 = main display, 2 = sub display). Commands without a suffix default to the main display.
  • Write responses: Non-query commands (those not ending in ?) return OK or nOK (firmware v25052021+).
  • Line terminator: Responses are terminated with . Commands should be sent with .

docs.title: Verified Commands
docs.text: Tested on firmware V4.3.0 via serial_query MCP tool.

docs.title: Device Identification
docs.text: Command Example Response Description *IDN? OWON,XDM1241,24412417,V4.3.0,3 Device identification (manufacturer, model, serial, firmware, unknown)

docs.title: Measurement
docs.text: Command Example Response Description MEAS? -2.758856E-05 Read main display value (shorthand for MEAS1?) MEAS1? -2.758856E-05 Read main display value MEAS2? NONe Read sub display value (NONe if sub display is off)

docs.title: Function / Mode
docs.text: Command Example Response Description FUNC? "VOLT" Current function on main display FUNC1? "VOLT" Current function on main display (explicit) FUNC2? NONe Current function on sub display

docs.title: Range
docs.text: Command Example Response Description RANGE1? 50 mV Current range on main display AUTO1? 1 Auto-range status on main display (1=on, 0=off)

docs.title: Sampling Rate
docs.text: Command Example Response Description RATE? S Sampling rate (S=Slow, M=Medium, F=Fast)

docs.title: Commands That Do NOT Work
docs.text:
These standard SCPI commands time out with no response:

  • MEAS:VOLT:DC? (standard SCPI measurement)
  • VOLT:RANG?, VOLT:RANG:AUTO?
  • SYST:ERR?
  • CONF?
  • RANG?
  • *RST (no response; may or may not execute)
  • VAL1?, VAL2?
  • RANGE2? (when sub display is off)

docs.title: Read voltage with serial_query
docs.text:
Tool: serial_query
Parameters:
  port: /dev/ttyUSB0
  command: MEAS1?
  baudrate: 115200
  timeout_ms: 3000

Response: {"response": "-2.758856E-05"}

docs.title: Identify the device
docs.text:
Tool: serial_query
Parameters:
  port: /dev/ttyUSB0
  command: *IDN?
  baudrate: 115200
  timeout_ms: 2000

Response: {"response": "OWON,XDM1241,24412417,V4.3.0,3"}

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• TheHWcave/OWON-XDM1041 - Community SCPI command list and firmware info
  • Elektroarzt/owon-xdm-remote - ESP32 remote control using SCPI over WiFi/MQTT
  • OWON XDM1000 Programming Manual - Official (incomplete) documentation
  • Owon XDM2041 - sigrok wiki - Related model in sigrok

docs.title: Firmware
docs.text: Some hardware drivers require firmware files that cannot be bundled with this server due to licensing restrictions.

docs.title: Which devices need firmware?
docs.text:
Devices that require firmware upload on connection include:

  • Kingst LA2016 / LA1016 / LA5016
  • Saleae Logic16
  • fx2lafw-based analyzers (many low-cost USB logic analyzers)

Devices that do not need firmware:

  • The demo virtual driver (always works)
  • Protocol-only analysis (decoding from files)
  • Some standalone instruments (SCPI multimeters, oscilloscopes)

docs.title: Checking firmware status
docs.text:
Use the check_firmware_status tool to verify firmware availability:

Tool: check_firmware_status

This reports which firmware directories exist and what files are present, helping diagnose device detection issues.

docs.title: Providing firmware (Docker)
docs.text:
Mount your firmware directory into the container:

docker run -i --rm --privileged \
  -v /path/to/sigrok-firmware:/usr/local/share/sigrok-firmware:ro \
  sigrok-mcp-server

docs.title: Where to get firmware
docs.text:
See the sigrok wiki on firmware for extraction instructions for your specific device. Each device has different firmware sources and extraction methods.

Note

Firmware files are typically extracted from vendor software installers. The sigrok wiki provides step-by-step instructions for each supported device.

docs.title: Docker (recommended)
docs.text:
Docker is the easiest way to get started — sigrok-cli and all dependencies are bundled in the image.

docker pull ghcr.io/kenosinc/sigrok-mcp-server
docker run -i --rm ghcr.io/kenosinc/sigrok-mcp-server

Tip

You can pin to a specific version tag (e.g. ghcr.io/kenosinc/sigrok-mcp-server:1.0) or use main for the latest development build.

docs.title: Building from Dockerfile
docs.text:
If you prefer to build the image yourself:

docker build -t sigrok-mcp-server .
docker run -i --rm sigrok-mcp-server

docs.title: From source
docs.text:
Requires:

  • Go 1.23+
  • sigrok-cli installed on your system (sigrok installation guide)
go build -o sigrok-mcp-server ./cmd/sigrok-mcp-server
./sigrok-mcp-server

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Configuration is done via environment variables:

Variable Default Description SIGROK_CLI_PATH sigrok-cli Path to the sigrok-cli binary SIGROK_TIMEOUT_SECONDS 30 Command execution timeout in seconds SIGROK_WORKING_DIR current dir Working directory for sigrok-cli execution

For Docker, pass environment variables with -e:

docker run -i --rm -e SIGROK_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=60 ghcr.io/kenosinc/sigrok-mcp-server

docs.title: USB device access (Docker)
docs.text:
To access USB-connected devices (logic analyzers, oscilloscopes, etc.) from inside the container:

docker run -i --rm --privileged ghcr.io/kenosinc/sigrok-mcp-server

Warning

--privileged grants broad access to host devices. For production use, consider using --device to pass specific USB devices instead.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: After installing the server, configure your MCP client to connect to it.

docs.title: Claude Desktop
docs.text:
Add to your claude_desktop_config.json:

BasicWith USB accessWith USB + firmware 
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "sigrok": {
      "command": "docker",
      "args": ["run", "-i", "--rm", "ghcr.io/kenosinc/sigrok-mcp-server"]
    }
  }
}

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "sigrok": {
      "command": "docker",
      "args": ["run", "-i", "--rm", "--privileged", "ghcr.io/kenosinc/sigrok-mcp-server"]
    }
  }
}

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "sigrok": {
      "command": "docker",
      "args": [
        "run", "-i", "--rm", "--privileged",
        "-v", "/path/to/sigrok-firmware:/usr/local/share/sigrok-firmware:ro",
        "ghcr.io/kenosinc/sigrok-mcp-server"
      ]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Claude Code
docs.text:
BasicWith USB + firmware 
claude mcp add sigrok -- docker run -i --rm ghcr.io/kenosinc/sigrok-mcp-server

claude mcp add sigrok -- docker run -i --rm --privileged \
  -v /path/to/sigrok-firmware:/usr/local/share/sigrok-firmware:ro \
  ghcr.io/kenosinc/sigrok-mcp-server

docs.title: Verify the connection
docs.text: Once configured, ask the LLM to run the show_version tool. You should see sigrok-cli and library version information returned, confirming the server is running correctly.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Browse available tools to see what you can do
  • Check the workflow example for a typical signal analysis session
  • If your device needs firmware, see the firmware guide

docs.title: Device not found
docs.text:
Symptom: scan_devices returns no devices.

Possible causes:

  1. USB not passed through (Docker): Use --privileged or --device flag.

    docker run -i --rm --privileged sigrok-mcp-server

  2. Missing firmware: Run check_firmware_status to verify. See the firmware guide.

  3. Permission denied: On Linux, ensure your user is in the plugdev group, or use udev rules for the device.

docs.title: Timeout errors
docs.text:
Symptom: Commands fail with a timeout error.

Possible causes:

  1. Slow device: Increase the timeout via environment variable:

    docker run -i --rm -e SIGROK_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=120 sigrok-mcp-server

  2. Serial timeout too short: For serial_query, increase timeout_ms (some instruments are slow to respond):

    Tool: serial_query
Parameters:
  port: /dev/ttyUSB0
  command: "*IDN?"
  timeout_ms: 5000

docs.title: Decoder produces no output
docs.text:
Symptom: decode_protocol returns empty results.

Possible causes:

  1. Wrong channel mapping: Ensure decoder channels match the capture channels. Use show_decoder_details to see required channels.

  2. Wrong baud rate / protocol settings: Verify settings match the actual signal.

  3. No transitions in data: The capture may not contain the expected signal activity.

docs.title: sigrok-cli not found
docs.text:
Symptom: Server fails to start or tools return "sigrok-cli not found" errors.

Possible causes:

  1. Not using Docker: The Docker image bundles sigrok-cli. If running from source, install it separately.

  2. Custom path: Set SIGROK_CLI_PATH if sigrok-cli is installed in a non-standard location.

docs.title: Serial port access denied
docs.text:
Symptom: serial_query returns a permission error.

Possible causes:

  1. Docker: The serial device must be passed through:

    docker run -i --rm --device=/dev/ttyUSB0 sigrok-mcp-server

  2. Linux permissions: Add your user to the dialout group:

    sudo usermod -a -G dialout $USER

docs.title: Workflow Example
docs.text: This guide walks through a typical signal analysis session using the MCP tools.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You have a logic analyzer connected via USB and want to capture and decode UART communication.

docs.title: Step 1: Scan for devices
docs.text:
First, discover what hardware is connected:

Tool: scan_devices

The LLM sees the connected logic analyzer and its driver ID.

docs.title: Step 2: Check driver capabilities
docs.text:
Inspect the driver to understand configuration options:

Tool: show_driver_details
Parameters:
  driver: fx2lafw

This reveals supported sample rates, channel counts, and other capabilities.

docs.title: Step 3: Capture data
docs.text:
Capture signal data from the device:

Tool: capture_data
Parameters:
  driver: fx2lafw
  channels: D0
  config: samplerate=1M
  samples: 1000000

The captured data is saved to a file.

docs.title: Step 4: Decode the protocol
docs.text:
Apply a UART decoder to the captured data:

Tool: decode_protocol
Parameters:
  input_file: capture_20260224_120000.sr
  protocol_decoders: uart:baudrate=9600:rx=D0

docs.title: Step 5: LLM analysis
docs.text:
The LLM receives the decoded output — individual bytes, frames, and timing information — and can:

  • Explain what the communication means
  • Identify protocol errors or anomalies
  • Suggest next steps (different baud rate, additional channels, stacked decoders)

docs.title: Working with serial instruments
docs.text:
For SCPI-capable instruments (multimeters, power supplies, etc.), you can communicate directly:

Tool: get_device_profile
Parameters:
  query: XDM1241

Then query the instrument using the profile's settings:

Tool: serial_query
Parameters:
  port: /dev/ttyUSB0
  command: "MEAS?"
  baudrate: 115200
  timeout_ms: 3000

docs.title: Tools Overview
docs.text: sigrok-mcp-server exposes the following MCP tools. Each tool translates into one or more sigrok-cli invocations and returns structured JSON results.

docs.title: Discovery tools
docs.text:
These tools query sigrok's capabilities and require no connected hardware.

Tool Description list_supported_hardware List all supported hardware drivers list_supported_decoders List all supported protocol decoders list_input_formats List all supported input file formats list_output_formats List all supported output file formats show_decoder_details Show detailed info about a protocol decoder show_driver_details Show detailed info about a hardware driver show_version Show sigrok-cli and library version info

docs.title: Device tools
docs.text:
These tools interact with connected hardware.

Tool Description scan_devices Scan for connected hardware devices capture_data Capture data from a device and save to file check_firmware_status Check firmware file availability

docs.title: Analysis tools
docs.text:
These tools process captured data.

Tool Description decode_protocol Decode protocol data from a captured file

docs.title: Instrument tools
docs.text:
These tools communicate directly with serial-attached instruments, independent of sigrok-cli.

Tool Description serial_query Send commands over a serial port get_device_profile Look up device profiles and connection settings

docs.title: capture_data
docs.text: Capture communication data from a connected device and save to file.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Type Required Description driver string Yes Hardware driver ID (e.g. fx2lafw, demo) conn string No Connection string for serial/network devices config string No Device configuration (e.g. samplerate=1M) channels string No Channels to use (e.g. D0,D1,D2) samples number No* Number of samples to acquire time number No* How long to sample in milliseconds triggers string No Trigger configuration (e.g. D0=r) wait_trigger boolean No Wait for trigger before capturing output_file string No Output filename (auto-generated if omitted)

*Either samples or time must be specified.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Information about the captured file, including the path to the saved data.

docs.title: Capture with the demo driver
docs.text:
Tool: capture_data
Parameters:
  driver: demo
  config: samplerate=1M
  samples: 1000000

docs.title: Capture from a real device with trigger
docs.text:
Tool: capture_data
Parameters:
  driver: fx2lafw
  channels: D0,D1
  config: samplerate=4M
  samples: 2000000
  triggers: D0=r
  wait_trigger: true

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The output file can be passed to decode_protocol for analysis.
  • If output_file is omitted, a filename is generated automatically.
  • Use scan_devices first to discover available devices and their drivers.

docs.title: check_firmware_status
docs.text: Check firmware file availability in standard sigrok firmware directories.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
Information about:

  • Which firmware directories exist
  • What firmware files are present in each directory
  • Overall firmware status

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Tool: check_firmware_status

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use this tool to diagnose device detection issues — missing firmware is the most common cause.
  • See the firmware guide for how to provide firmware files.

docs.title: decode_protocol
docs.text: Decode protocol data from a captured file using sigrok protocol decoders.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description input_file string Yes Input filename protocol_decoders string Yes Protocol decoders to apply (e.g. uart:baudrate=9600) input_format string No Input format (e.g. vcd, binary) annotations string No Decoder annotation filter (e.g. uart=rx-data) show_sample_numbers boolean No Include sample numbers in output meta_output string No Decoder meta output filter (e.g. uart=baud) json_trace boolean No Output in Google Trace Event JSON format

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Decoded protocol data from the file.

docs.title: Decode UART data
docs.text:
Tool: decode_protocol
Parameters:
  input_file: capture.sr
  protocol_decoders: uart:baudrate=9600:rx=D0

docs.title: Decode SPI with annotation filter
docs.text:
Tool: decode_protocol
Parameters:
  input_file: capture.sr
  protocol_decoders: spi:clk=D0:mosi=D1:miso=D2:cs=D3
  annotations: spi=mosi-data

docs.title: Decode with JSON trace output
docs.text:
Tool: decode_protocol
Parameters:
  input_file: capture.sr
  protocol_decoders: i2c:scl=D0:sda=D1
  json_trace: true

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use show_decoder_details to see available options and channels for a decoder.
  • Decoder options are specified inline with the decoder ID, separated by colons (e.g. uart:baudrate=115200:rx=D0).
  • Files are captured using capture_data or imported from external sources.

docs.title: get_device_profile
docs.text: Look up a device profile by name, model, manufacturer, or *IDN? response string. Returns connection settings, supported commands with examples, and device-specific notes.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description query string Yes Device name, model, manufacturer, or *IDN? response string to match against

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
Device profile including:

  • Connection settings (baudrate, parity, etc.)
  • Supported commands with examples
  • Device-specific notes and limitations

docs.title: Look up by model name
docs.text:
Tool: get_device_profile
Parameters:
  query: XDM1241

docs.title: Look up by IDN response
docs.text:
Tool: get_device_profile
Parameters:
  query: "OWON,XDM1241,24412417,V4.3.0,3"

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use this tool before serial_query to get the correct settings for a device.
  • Profiles are embedded in the server; see devices for currently supported profiles.

docs.title: list_input_formats
docs.text: List all supported input file formats.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: An array of {id, description} objects, one per supported input format.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Tool: list_input_formats

Response (excerpt):

[
  {"id": "binary", "description": "Raw binary logic data"},
  {"id": "vcd", "description": "Value Change Dump data"},
  {"id": "wav", "description": "WAV file"}
]

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: • Input formats determine what file types can be used with decode_protocol.

docs.title: list_output_formats
docs.text: List all supported output file formats.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: An array of {id, description} objects, one per supported output format.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Tool: list_output_formats

Response (excerpt):

[
  {"id": "srzip", "description": "sigrok session file format data"},
  {"id": "csv", "description": "Comma-separated values"},
  {"id": "vcd", "description": "Value Change Dump data"}
]

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: • Output formats determine the file format used by capture_data.

docs.title: list_supported_decoders
docs.text: List all supported protocol decoders.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: An array of {id, description} objects, one per supported protocol decoder.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Tool: list_supported_decoders

Response (excerpt):

[
  {"id": "uart", "description": "Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter"},
  {"id": "spi", "description": "Serial Peripheral Interface"},
  {"id": "i2c", "description": "Inter-Integrated Circuit"}
]

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This is a read-only query that does not require connected hardware.
  • Use show_decoder_details to get full information about a specific decoder.

docs.title: list_supported_hardware
docs.text: List all supported hardware drivers.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: An array of {id, description} objects, one per supported hardware driver.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Tool: list_supported_hardware

Response (excerpt):

[
  {"id": "demo", "description": "Demo driver and target"},
  {"id": "fx2lafw", "description": "fx2lafw (generic driver for FX2 based LAs)"},
  {"id": "kingst-la2016", "description": "Kingst LA2016"}
]

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This is a read-only query that does not require connected hardware.
  • The list comes from the sigrok-cli installation inside the container.

docs.title: scan_devices
docs.text: Scan for connected hardware devices.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
All parameters are optional. When no parameters are provided, a broad scan is performed.

Parameter Type Required Description driver string No Hardware driver ID for targeted scanning (e.g. scpi-dmm, rigol-ds) conn string No Connection string for targeted scanning (e.g. /dev/ttyUSB0:serialcomm=115200/8n1)

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
An object with:

  • devices — Array of {driver, description} objects for detected devices
  • warnings — Firmware-related issues for devices that could not be initialized
  • hint — Suggestions for resolving issues

docs.title: Broad scan
docs.text: Tool: scan_devices

docs.title: Targeted scan for a serial instrument
docs.text:
Tool: scan_devices
Parameters:
  driver: scpi-dmm
  conn: /dev/ttyUSB0:serialcomm=115200/8n1

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Broad scans may take several seconds depending on available drivers.
  • Warnings about firmware typically indicate that a device was detected but its firmware files are missing. See the firmware guide.
  • SCPI driver categories: scpi-dmm (multimeters), scpi-pps (power supplies), or vendor-specific like rigol-ds (oscilloscopes).

docs.title: serial_query
docs.text: Send a command string over a serial port and return the device response. Works with any serial-attached instrument that accepts text commands (e.g. SCPI). Independent of sigrok-cli.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description port string Yes Serial device path (e.g. /dev/ttyUSB0) command string Yes Command to send (e.g. *IDN?, MEAS:VOLT:DC?) baudrate number No Baud rate (default: 9600) databits number No Data bits: 5, 6, 7, or 8 (default: 8) parity string No Parity: none, odd, even, mark, space (default: none) stopbits string No Stop bits: 1, 1.5, 2 (default: 1) timeout_ms number No Read timeout in milliseconds (default: 1000)

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: The device response string.

docs.title: Identify a device
docs.text:
Tool: serial_query
Parameters:
  port: /dev/ttyUSB0
  command: "*IDN?"
  baudrate: 115200
  timeout_ms: 3000

docs.title: Read a voltage measurement
docs.text:
Tool: serial_query
Parameters:
  port: /dev/ttyUSB0
  command: "MEAS:VOLT:DC?"
  baudrate: 9600

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This tool communicates directly over serial and does not use sigrok-cli.
  • Use get_device_profile to look up correct connection settings before querying an unknown device.
  • See device documentation for device-specific command references.

docs.title: show_decoder_details
docs.text: Show detailed information about a specific protocol decoder, including options, channels, annotation classes, and documentation.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description decoder string Yes Protocol decoder ID (e.g. uart, spi, i2c)

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
Detailed information about the decoder including:

  • Description and documentation
  • Required and optional channels
  • Configurable options with defaults
  • Annotation classes (output categories)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Tool: show_decoder_details
Parameters:
  decoder: uart

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use list_supported_decoders to discover available decoder IDs.
  • Decoder options (e.g. baudrate, parity) are passed via the protocol_decoders parameter in decode_protocol.

docs.title: show_driver_details
docs.text: Show detailed information about a specific hardware driver, including supported functions, scan options, and connected devices.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description driver string Yes Hardware driver ID (e.g. demo, fx2lafw, rigol-ds)

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
Detailed information about the driver including:

  • Supported functions and capabilities
  • Scan options (connection parameters, serial settings)
  • Detected devices (if any are connected)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Tool: show_driver_details
Parameters:
  driver: fx2lafw

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use list_supported_hardware to discover available driver IDs.
  • This tool may trigger a device scan for the specified driver.

docs.title: show_version
docs.text: Show sigrok-cli version information, including library versions.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
Version information for:

  • sigrok-cli
  • libsigrok
  • libsigrokdecode

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Tool: show_version

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Useful for verifying the server is running and checking library versions.
  • This is a good first tool to call when testing a new connection.
